data.title: Salesforce Is A Great Long-Term Buy
data.description:
Salesforce (CRM) has grossly underperformed the overall market in 2025. At the time of writing, it lost about 30% of its value, compared to a gain of 16% for the S&P 500 (SPY), and almost 22% for the mighty Magnificent 7 (MAGS). One could speculate that such performance disconnect might be due to an eroding segment of the business, a company suffering from a high valuation, future performance uncertainties, or a vulnerable moat. However, I do not see any major disruption in Salesforce's numbers, and I believe the negative sentiment against the business is undeserved.

I have published about Salesforce before. And not much has changed in my thesis except for the stock price. I believe Salesforce is a high quality business that is poised to benefit from the AI and productivity boom. It is also trading at one of its lowest valuations in years on an earnings per share and a free cash flow basis. In fact, its forward price-to-earnings, which ranges between 17 to 19, is lower than the S&P 500, which stands at 22.6. Since I published the first article about Salesforce, the stock has dropped about 7% while the overall market increased 5%. I believe that the risk/reward of investing in Salesforce is positively skewed in my favor at this point, and I am buying as many shares of I can of the stock.

Past Results

Salesforce last reported quarterly results on September 3, 2025. Management announced another quarter of strong results. The revenue reached an all-time-high at $10.24B, representing a 10% year-over-year growth (or 9% in constant currency). Current Remaining Performance Obligations (cRPO) increased at a higher rate than the revenue, at 11% YoY or 10% in constant currency, indicating that revenue will potentially accelerate one or two digits within the next 12 months.

While total revenue growth was 10% YoY in their latest quarters, two segments of the business were growing at a faster rate. Platform & Other grew 16% YoY in constant currency, and Integration & Analytics grew 12%. These are the two pillars that heavily rely on AI and which are accelerating Salesforce's revenue. Platform & Other is the core software infrastructure, which includes the Salesforce Agentic Enterprise suite. During the conference call, management mentioned

We have our metadata platform unifying our apps, our data and agents into one powerful agentic operating system. We are rebuilding every single one of our products to be agentic. We're delivering almost every single one of those products at Dreamforce.

Integration & Analytics contains products such as Data Cloud, Tableau, and soon Informatica. Data Cloud provides data, metadata and context to Salesforce's AI agents, while Tableau is used to pre-process the data and present it to the user. Clean and usable data is imperative to train AI models. Management realizes that

It's not really spoken for some reason by others, but if you don't have your data right, you don't get your AI right. And so, we all understand that, and we think that every customer is going to need an Informatica.

There is no question that Salesforce is benefitting from AI. Don't believe me? Here are some additional revenue growth numbers

Quarter Platform & Other Integration & Analytics
Q1 2024 12% 20%
Q2 2024 11% 16%
Q3 2024 11% 22%
Q4 2024 10% 21%
Q1 2025 10% 25%
Q2 2025 10% 14%
Q3 2025 8% 5%
Q4 2025 12% 6%

Q1 2026

 14% 10%

Q2 2026

 16% 12%

Since Q3 2025 (the company's fiscal year runs from February 1st to January 31st, and they will report earnings for their Q3 2026 on December 3, 2025), the revenue growth has doubled for both segments.

Operating cash flow, free cash flow, and earnings per share (diluted) in their most recent quarter were also strong, coming in at $0.74B, $0.6B, and $2.91. However, keep in mind that the stock-based compensation program, with an expense of $0.793B or $0.82 per share, ate a significant part of the company's (and the shareholder's) profits. Management is guiding for a stock-based compensation expense of about 8.4% of the total revenues for their fiscal year 2026, which might be close to $3.44B, approximately $200M of what they spent last year. The stock-based compensation expense is a little bit high for my taste.

One last thing to mention: the company is loaded with cash, giving management the ability to navigate economic through economic uncertainty.

The company had $10.37B in cash and cash equivalents and $5B in marketable securities, which include corporate debt notes, U.S. treasuries and asset-backed securities, for the most part, which provided the company with $0.181B in interest income during the quarter. Salesforce has $8.5B in long-term debt, of which $2.5B are due in fiscal year 2029, and $6.0B are due after fiscal 2030, giving management ample room for acquisitions, capex investments and share buybacks.

What To Expect When The Company Announces Earnings

A few notable events happened since Salesforce released their latest earnings report on 9/3/2025, and I expect them to continue driving revenue growth. Investors will be paying close attention to management's comments on these. Below is a summary of the most notable developments:

Release of Agentforce 360 (10/13/2025)

The company released Agentforce 360 on 10/13/2025. The products features

  • Agentforce 360 Platform: enterprise AI agents, which now have conversational building, hybrid reasoning and voice capabilities.
  • Data 360: data layer that provides each AI agent with the context.
  • Customer 360 Apps: business KPIs such as product selling metadata, markets, etc.
  • Slack: interface to communicate with the company team and AI agents.

Investors are referred to the original press note by Salesforce regarding Agentforce 360. I expect this software suite to streamline processes and drive additional revenue for the company as a one stop shop for customer relationship management platforms.

Williams-Sonoma deployed Agentforce 360 across its brand portfolio on 10/14/2025, just one day after it was released. A major win for Salesforce.

An Update On Future Revenues (10/15/2025)

Salesforce announced and updated their long-term revenue target. For fiscal year 2030, the company is expecting $60B+ in revenue, a 10% organic growth from fiscal year 2026 to 2030. Management is expecting AI to boost the revenue growth. They disclosed that its total agentic AI annual recurring revenue (ARR) as of Q2/2026 was approximately $440M. Investors will be expecting updates on this number.

Strategic Partnerships (10/14/2025 & 10/16/2025)

The company announced partnerships with OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google (Gemini) on 10/14/2025 and 10/16/2025, respectively.

Salesforce will let companies access its Agentforce 360 in ChatGPT. They will also allow the usage of OpenAI's latest models, including GPT-5, to build AI agents and prompts within the Salesforce platform. This partnership offers flexibility to Salesforce's customers and opens the platform to be more versatile. The company also announced that they will implement Google's Gemini models into the Agentforce 360 platform. Customers can now use Gemini models for Salesforce's Atlas Reasoning Engine, also enabling them to deploy AI agents in Salesforce's platform. Finally, Anthropic's models are also available in the Agentforce 360 platform as of now.

These partnerships are key for the long-term sustained growth of Salesforce. I believe utilizing the best technology in your products by partnering with other companies is the right approach, rather than building a new LLM from scratch that might not be as powerful as the industry-leading models. I expect this to further drive revenue growth for the company.

Informatica Acquisition (11/18/2025)

The company announced that they completed the Informatica acquisition as of 11/18/2025. This is a huge step for Salesforce, as the Informatica technology will enable the company to pre-process and work with the raw data before it is used for training models and drawing projections. Investors will want to hear more about how they are merging Informatica with Agentforce 360 and how it will be used to drive future revenue growth.

To summarize, Wall Street will be closely watching for any acceleration of AI-driven growth and margin expansion in the next earnings call, and commentary for all the previously-mentioned posts. Current Remaining Performance Obligations should meet or exceed the guidance of slightly above 10% growth YoY provided by management, as this is the best indication of acceleration of future revenue growth. Investors will be expecting that the company meets or exceeds their previous quarterly revenue guidance of $10.24B to $10.29B. Of course, any acceleration on the Platform & Other, and the Integration & Analytics segments will further substantiate the AI-fueled growth narrative. Wall Street will watch how the partnerships are helping the AI-fueled narrative.

Investors should also monitor the Data Cloud and AI Annual Recurring Revenue for continued and strong acceleration. During the last earnings call, Data Cloud and AI ARR reached $1.2B, growing a whopping 120% YoY. In addition, expect some comments regarding the adoption of Agentforce. Wall Street will want to see the rate at which customers are increasing their investments in Agentforce and the growth in the number of pad Agentforce deals.

Where I Could Be Wrong

Investors should consider that investing in Salesforce can be risky. For once, the company's top and bottom line would take a hit shall we enter in a recession in 2026, as they rely on their customers spending on CRM software, which could be cut. Secondly, the AI thesis could prove to be wrong; the company might only achieve mediocre revenue growth rates in the future as other companies take their market share. Finally, investors should also pay close attention to any details regarding the Informatica acquisition, as delays will impact the company's growth rates in the short term.

Conclusion

I believe Salesforce will benefit from AI, and some metrics are already showing an acceleration. The company is trading at one of its lowest valuations, and lower than the overall market. Their business model is predictable and I am buying as many shares as I can at this price.

data.title: United Natural Foods Q1 Preview: Doesn't Seem Like An Exciting Opportunity Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

United Natural Foods (UNFI) is due to report its Q1 ’26 numbers on December 2nd, so I wanted to give a sense of what to expect and what I am looking for from the company’s operations in 2026. Right now, the momentum doesn’t seem to be on the company’s side. For this reason, I will remain on the sidelines until I see an improvement in the conventional products section and natural foods regain their double-digit growth.

What to Expect

In terms of earnings, analysts are expecting the company to bring in around $0.40 and $0.27 a share on non-GAAP and GAAP, respectively, on around $7.91B in sales. That translates to around 3% growth sequentially, and essentially flat (+.5%) y/y. The company didn’t provide any specifics regarding the upcoming quarter and focused primarily on the full-year outlook, targeting $31.6B-$32B in sales, but I don’t expect to see much deviation in Q1 from analysts’ expectations. For the last ten quarters, the company managed to beat expectations 50% of the time on sales, while beating on earnings almost 100% of the time, so it is literally a coin flip when it comes to the company’s top-line performance, and I’m leaning more towards a miss this time around. On the EPS side, I’m more confident because the beats were much more convincing. Additionally, there were 6 upward revisions for EPS in the last 90 days.

Overall, I don’t think it is going to be a very different quarter from the rest. Miss or beat estimates, the company’s share price experienced quite massive swings on the day of the report. UNFI’s share price has been very volatile over the past year, so I expect big swings in either direction.

What I’m Looking For

The management did some reorganizing of the company’s revenue segments since I last covered it in February. The management realigned its wholesale business recently into two product-centered divisions. Conventional grocery products and Natural products. The Supernatural category, which was essentially UNFI’s Whole Foods channel, is now embedded within the new “Natural” segment. This is going to be harder to track now, since the Supernatural category was the only category that saw any decent growth in the recent past, but it makes the financial reports much easier to track when there are fewer categories to look at.

So, I will largely be focusing on the company’s Natural products segment, as I believe this is where the company will see more growth because of Whole Foods within it. Conventional will most likely come in flat or even decline, while in Natural products, I expect at least a high-single-digit contribution to the top-line performance.

In the Q4’25 earnings report, the company’s gross margins suffered slightly due to lower product margin rates and customer and product mix, and LIFO accounting, which, in an inflationary period of increasing costs, brought the company’s gross margins down by around 10 bps. Its operating margins took a hit due to the cybersecurity incident, which amounted to elevated shrinkage and operational inefficiencies (manual processes, spoilage, and overtime), costing the company sales.

I will be looking out for how margins progressed over the last three months since Q4. I expect to see some improvement, although it will not be a big one in my view, since the company operates in a rather tight-margin business. Any improvement will be positive.

Over the past year, the company’s wholesale unit’s volume trend has been positive, with the small exception in Q4 due to the mentioned cyberattack, which cost the company sales, with an implied volume drag of around 5 points. Before the incident, we could clearly see the improvement in wholesale volume up until Q3, so I will be looking out for the trend to return, and if it doesn’t, then I would like to hear a good reason as to why the trend turned negative. The management could still say it is due to the cyberattack, but we will have to wait until the conference call or a press release on the day of earnings.

Regarding the company’s other business segment, Conventional products, which has been dragging UNFI’s overall performance down quite substantially, as it declined 6% in Q4 and was flat in 2025 overall. I would like to hear what the management is going to do to revitalize this segment in 2026, so that it complements the Natural Foods segment instead of dragging it down. The management said that they are looking to streamline processes across their distribution centers, targeting around $4B in operating spend to lower unit costs and to improve service levels for conventional customers. This probably won’t rejuvenate its top-line performance, but what that will do is improve the segment operationally, which will be more beneficial, in my opinion. I would like to hear how the progress has been going on this initiative and the progress on pushing private brands and data-driven revenue management that helps conventional retailers differentiate on value instead of just price. This would lead to a better price mix overall.

In terms of the company’s financials, if operations improve substantially over the next few quarters, which is still in the air, I would like to see even more progress on reducing its long-term debt pile, which as of Q4’25 stood at $1.86B, down from $2.081B. The management has been vocal about the debt reduction, and I don’t think that is going to change in the upcoming report. Tackling the debt situation will attract more investors in the end and will leave the company with more resources to grow the business rather than allocating some of it towards debt obligations like interest payments, which were substantial, especially when the company was not profitable this past year.

Lastly, which is probably the most important part of any earnings report, is what the company is going to do with the guidance it provided already in Q4 ’25. Any revisions to the downside are going to wreak havoc on the company’s shares. I don’t expect the company to come out with a downward revision, but if things are not improving, the Q2 earnings report might have some sort of a revision.

Closing Comments

The company’s potential lies within the management’s ability to revitalize the company’s conventional products segments, as well as maintain the positive momentum in the Natural foods segment, which has not been growing as much as it used to, which adds more worries to the company’s operations overall.

I don’t think we will see much changing over the next couple of quarters, and without the evidence that the company’s cost-cutting initiatives and growth projects are working, I will remain on the sidelines. It just doesn’t seem exciting to me right now.

data.title: DIV: The 7% Yield Comes With Some Serious Drawbacks
data.description:
In October, I covered the Global X SuperDividend ETF (SDIV). My view on that fund wasn't positive; I suggested that its strategy wasn't super.

My conclusion on SDIV ran as follows:

"I'm not opposed to the idea of buying high-yielding securities. But I think you need more filtering and selection criteria than simply the yield alone. Otherwise, you're going to end up owning a lot of value traps and have a great deal of portfolio turnover when a substantial number of these firms invariably reduce their payouts.

You can do well owning a 10% dividend yield security here or there where conditions are favorable or the market is getting its risk perception wrong. A portfolio of a hundred securities that yield 10% on average, however, seems bound to underperform over the long run."

Sometimes, when building a portfolio, geography can make a big difference.

While I don't think SDIV's global dividend strategy is likely to produce alpha, perhaps a similar model could work better in the United States. This brings us to the fund's U.S.-focused counterpart, the Global X SuperDividend U.S. ETF (NYSEARCA:DIV).

The U.S. version of this fund has a relatively similar strategy to the global version, building a portfolio based primarily on owning stocks with highest overall dividend yields. However, DIV limits its eligibility to U.S.-listed companies. In addition, it is more concentrated, as it has 50 holdings as compared to SDIV's 100 stock portfolio. Finally, DIV has a low-beta filter as well, meaning it prioritizes stocks which display lower realized volatility than the overall S&P 500.

How has this translated into total returns? The U.S. SuperDividend ETF is up 63%, including dividends, since 2013 while the global SuperDividend fund is up just 3%:

You could explain away the performance difference due to the fact that U.S. stocks, have, in general, dramatically outperformed global markets over the past decade. That said, both funds are heavily tilted to the value factor, whereas U.S. outperformance of recent years has been largely driven by the rise of the Mag 7 and other fast-growing tech stocks which would be outside the investment mandate of either of these funds.

Granted, some of DIV's outperformance versus SDIV is likely due to the U.S. market's overall strength. However, I think there's another factor that gives DIV a shot at better long-term performance as well.

Lower But Potentially More Sustainable Yield

A key difference between these funds is that the U.S. counterpart tends to offer a lower overall yield than the global version. See this chart of both funds' trailing dividend yields since inception:

DIV did have a higher yield than SDIV for a brief period in 2015-16. But, on average, DIV typically has a much lower yield than its global counterpart. Excluding the pandemic period, DIV has frequently yielded around 7%, which is where it is again today.

It seems that, at least in current market conditions, there simply aren't as many double-digit yielding stocks located in the U.S. as opposed to global markets. And the low-volatility filter is likely screening out some of the riskier higher-yielding U.S. income stocks as well.

Why does that matter?

While there are always exceptions, in general, it's hard for companies to sustain double-digit dividend yields for long. Typically, a company's prospects will improve and the share price will rebound, which pushes the yield down. Or the company will have to slash its dividend. It's rare for a company to have a sustainable 12% yield, for example. Usually, it gets that high because the company is facing a crisis of some nature.

In my experience, there is a significantly higher chance that a company yielding in the 6-8% range is simply down on its luck at the moment or in the bad part of an industry cycle rather than facing an existential crisis. I've seen a fair number of companies have their dividend yield rise to, say, 7% over the years and been able to keep the payout intact. Whereas, if the yield blows out to well into the double-digits, quite often, the dividend is going to get slashed.

How has this played out in practice? Here is a chart showing the total change in each funds' dividend payout per share during the time when these two ETFs have both been operating:

The global version, SDIV, has seen its dividend per share fall 56% over that period, with the dividend now sitting at historical lows. DIV's payout per share has dropped 30% over the same time period, which admittedly isn't great either. However, of note, the DIV's payout has been stable since the pandemic and has actually gone up over the past year.

Put another way, perhaps DIV's dividend is sustainable around current levels, at least in a normal economy. Though in a recession, all bets are off. But from a baseline perspective, DIV seems like it has a much better shot of being able to support its payout as opposed to SDIV.

Holdings & Sector Composition

As of this writing, DIV's top ten holdings are as follows:

  • Global Ship Lease (GSL)
  • Ardagh Metal Packaging (AMBP)
  • NorthWestern Energy Group (NWE)
  • Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI)
  • Northwest Natural Holding (NWN)
  • Spire Inc. (SR)
  • National Health Investors (NHI)
  • Evergy Inc (EVRG)
  • Avista Corp (AVA)
  • Flex Lng (FLNG)

There are some seemingly risky dividends here. Industries such as shipping and LNG have historically had highly volatile earnings profiles, which can impact dividend sustainability. And the No. 2 largest holding, Ardagh Metal Packaging, is currently paying a dividend that is much larger than its projected forward earnings.

On balance, however, the top 10 positions are a collection of fairly sleepy conservative companies in sectors such as utilities and REITs that tend to have reasonably secure dividends.

And here's how DIV's holdings break out by sector:

  • Real Estate: 21.7%
  • Utilities: 21.2%
  • Energy: 17.3%
  • Consumer Defensive: 10.5%
  • Industrials: 9.3%
  • Basic Material: 5.9%
  • Communication: 4.8%
  • Financials: 4.1%
  • Consumer Cyclical: 3.1%
  • Health Care: 2.2%
  • Technology: 0.0%

Like with its global counterpart, DIV is also highly-reliant on just a few sectors. Real estate, utilities, and energy collectively make up well over half of the overall portfolio.

There's a chance this will work out fine for DIV owners. I wouldn't be surprised if real estate outperforms over the next few years. We appear to be in a significant rate cutting cycle, and REITs should benefit if capital gets cheaper again. Utilities generally have defensible dividends and should benefit in a rate-cutting environment. There's also some potential upside from AI-driven power demand.

As for energy, things aren't looking great for oil prices recently. But energy stocks often trade up to higher dividend yields without necessarily leading to cuts. During the pandemic, for example, even blue-chip energy stocks like Exxon Mobil (XOM) saw their yields spike up to as high as 10%. I'd much prefer to have an overweight energy position with the price of oil in the $80s rather than struggling to hold $60 per barrel. But as long as oil stabilizes fairly soon, perhaps energy stocks will be able to maintain their current dividends as well.

Based on the top three sectors, DIV has a decent shot at maintaining its current dividend payout as long as the economy doesn't get too bad.

However, I doubt DIV will see much dividend growth going forward, either. These three sectors that DIV has concentrated in generally have fairly plodding dividend growth rates even in good times. And there's some selection bias here as well; DIV is buying higher-yielding REITs, utilities, etc. that likely have more limited growth upside within their respective industries.

Meanwhile, DIV has almost no exposure to healthcare or technology, which by all accounts are likely to be two of the more promising growth sectors within the U.S. economy in coming years.

Global X SuperDividend U.S. ETF Bottom Line

I do think that DIV is a moderately more attractive ETF than its global counterpart. More of the fund appears to be invested in the sorts of stable REITs and utility stocks that are likely to have sustained dividend prospects for many years to come. On the flip side of that coin, there appear to be fewer clear value traps that are likely to end up with dividend cuts and significant losses of invested capital over time. One other positive is that DIV's expense ratio of 0.45% comes in at 13 basis points cheaper than SDIV's fee.

Is this enough to rate DIV shares as a buy?

In my view, no.

I would expect DIV's total return to be no more than the current dividend yield going forward, so 6.9% as of this writing. And that's probably an optimistic forecast.

DIV's share price has actually declined fairly consistently, albeit slowly, since inception:

And that during a massive U.S. bull market, no less.

If DIV is able to maintain something close to the current dividend but the share price continues to slowly decline over time, those total return forecasts dip into the low single digits annualized range. And in a recession/bear market scenario where a significant chunk of DIV's portfolio slashes dividends, the floor could fall out on this ETF.

All this adds up to a sell rating in my book. I think most investors would be better-served owning a more diversified dividend ETF. I get the appeal of DIV's higher starting payout of the gate. But with such limited prospects for either dividend growth or share price appreciation over time, the fund's income stream is not nearly as strong as it might first seem.

data.title: Why Customer's Stickiness Is The Real Growth Engine Behind Celestica
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The present article highlights why Celestica (NYSE: CLS) is, in my view, a solid buy, based on the customized solutions it offers across both of its segments, Advanced Technology Solutions - ATS - and Connectivity & Cloud Solutions - CCS.

Its customized solutions across a very wide range of industries, combined with the AI-related demand for its tech-edge Hardware Platform Solutions - HPS - drive its long-term growth.

However, before continuing with my thoughts, I would like to make clear that CLS has to deal with the risk of high revenue concentration in a small number of clients and low margins. Therefore, its risk-reward profile is not very attractive for defensive investors, but based on its business model and tech-edge position, I believe it can outperform in the long run

Financials Analysis

Starting with revenue growth, the company shows an upward trend from FY22, along with even stronger profit growth, signaling that its profitability depends on scaling — meaning more products sold, lower cost per unit, and higher profits.

Its total debt in FY24 was $932M, with the majority being long-term debt, which is a positive factor considering that CLS is not struggling to meet its short-term obligations, especially given its strong cash position. The leverage ratios of debt/equity and debt/FCF have a downward trend since FY23, indicating an improving financial position.

Notably, the interest coverage of 11.6 in FY24, which shows how many times the company can cover its interest payments, supports the company’s growth outlook. However, as already mentioned, CLS has low margins. Despite the solid revenue and profit growth in absolute terms, its profit margin remains at low levels compared to the broader industry.

Starting from FY22 again, all the company’s margins show improvement, but not at levels that significantly reshape its financial position. I expect relatively low profit margins, especially during this period in which the company is investing heavily in capex and restructuring its ATS segment, but an FCF margin of only 3% is a real risk concern, in my view.

I'm concerned not only for valuation purposes, but even more because low FCF cannot adequately support future investments in R&D, new products, or facility expansions—both crucial for the company’s long-term performance—and this is, in my view, a true risk.

According to the company:

While capital expenditures year-to-date were $107 million and also 1.2% of revenue. Next year, capital expenditures are expected to rise to between 2.0% and 2.5% of revenue. So we're on track on just under $200 million of capex this year. We're anticipating right now somewhere between $300 million and $400 million of capex next year. We have consistently grown our investments in R&D over the years. We increased our spend more than 50% this year, and we expect at least a 50% increase in 2026 in support of new program wins that will ramp beginning over the next two years.

Based on the company’s guidance, both capex and R&D will see a significant increase—primarily R&D. In my view, R&D is a critical aspect for Celestica, as it underpins the company’s ability to maintain its leading position in the switching market (with a 41% share), as well as to support ASIC integration, hardware design, and engineering for ATS custom products (which I will explain further later).

Despite its weak FCF margin, Celestica’s main revenue growth catalyst—the HPS product line—grew 85% YTD to approximately $3.6B compared to YTD 2024, accounting for 42% of total YTD 2025 revenue. At the same time, the company invested only $120M to support this growth. In other words, Celestica is generating substantial expansion with minimal capital, a very positive signal for its long-term growth and future FCF improvement.

Overall, the company seems to have an upward trend in revenue, profitability, and margins, along with a decrease in leverage ratios. However, despite this trend, its margins remain at very low levels, with the FCF margin being my primary risk concern — specifically regarding how the company can effectively and cost-efficiently support its growth trajectory without compromising profitability.

Customized Solutions Increase Customer Stickiness

The ATS segment is a diversified, engineering-heavy business unit serving industries that require custom hardware and complex solutions, such as Aerospace & Defense (A&D), HealthTech, Industrial, and Capital Equipment.

CCS is the second segment, producing products for networking, telecommunications, servers, and cloud infrastructure — essentially everything related to data center infrastructure, excluding the very high-end AI/HPS stack.

HPS is not an official segment but deserves to be assessed separately, given that it serves as the company’s primary growth catalyst. HPS is included within the CCS segment. The HPS business designs and manufactures advanced AI/ML compute racks, leading-edge Ethernet switches, and creates custom "end-to-end" AI platforms for hyperscalers.

As you may notice, Celestica designs and develops customized solutions across both of its segments. ATS represents its traditional customized revenue stream, while CCS offers customized solutions for AI players in combination with non-customized solutions such as routers and optical network equipment. However, the company’s main growth driver, HPS, focuses exclusively on customized solutions.

Let’s examine how Celestica leverages customer stickiness and the benefits this approach may provide.

ATS Segment

Starting with the company’s statements:

Revenue in our ATS segment for the quarter was $781 million, down 4%, slightly lower than our guidance of a low single-digit percentage decline. The lower performance year-over-year was primarily driven by portfolio reshaping in our A&D business as discussed in past quarters. Moving on to segment margins. ATS segment margin in the quarter was 5.5%, up 60 basis points, primarily driven by improved profitability in our A&D business. ... In our ATS segment, we anticipate revenue to be down in the low single-digit percentage range… or we said going into 2026, it's going to be low single digits… After exiting 2024 with a segment margin of 4.6%, we have already improved to 5.5% in the third quarter of 2025 and expect to achieve 70 basis points of full year margin expansion.

In short, Celestica anticipates flat revenue growth for FY25 and mid single-digit growth for FY26. The company has reshaped this segment to focus on the most profitable, higher-margin projects. As a result, the segment’s operating margin has already improved, and the company expects a margin of approximately 6.25% for FY25.

But why is this relatively flat-growth segment considered a growth catalyst? The answer lies in the company’s business reshaping. CLS targets complex projects, which is why it maintains a large engineering team to provide consulting, design, and ultimately develop the products that customers need.

Industries such as A&D and HealthTech involve long-cycle projects that require significant financial and human resources to produce hardware. Therefore, with 1,100 design engineers, supporting both hardware and software solutions, CLS can offer solutions at every step of production — from design through manufacturing and aftersales.

As a result of the complexity of these products and the high switching costs, CLS’ customers are more “sticky,” meaning the company enjoys more stable and predictable revenue streams. Additionally, in my view, the more involved the company’s engineers are in the production process, the greater the value provided to customers, which can allow CLS to charge more over time — ultimately enhancing the segment’s profitability and margins.

CCS Segment

CCS is the main revenue segment, expected to produce $9B in FY25, compared to ATS at $3.2B in the same period. This segment has strong momentum for further expansion, with the company projecting revenue growth of 40% in FY26. The main drivers of this growth are continued high demand from hyperscalers for computing power and AI infrastructure, which translates into demand for rack-scale AI/ML computing systems and high-speed switches. CLS is a leader in the switch market, with 800G and 1.6T switches as its flagship products.

Additionally, the company anticipates a ramp-up starting in 2H26, as it will begin production of rack-scale custom AI systems for a digital native client. CLS is capable of designing and producing custom ASIC solutions for its AI-related clients. As with ATS, custom products create greater customer stickiness. With the HPS product line — which provides full rack-scale AI/ML compute systems and specialized end-to-end custom solutions — this stickiness is even more pronounced.

I believe the company’s strong momentum in the CCS-HPS segment, the stickiness of its solutions, and the ATS segment reshaping can easily lead to higher margin expansion than CLS’ guidance. Specifically, CLS anticipates 40 basis points of operating margin expansion for 2026, compared to an expected FY25 margin of 7.4%, and I think this target is too conservative.

As the company’s CFO stated:

…you're putting 40% growth on the CCS business, maintaining the margins that CCS has and maintaining the margin that ATS has will yield that 40 basis points improvement. We are working towards expanding margins in both businesses, and we do believe that, that is an opportunity.

I interpret this to mean that the company itself expects — unofficially — further margin expansion in the CCS segment, implying a potential overall beat of margin guidance.

Valuation and Risk Analysis

My valuation analysis relies on three main factors: Valuation metrics, revenue growth, and profitability, compared to its main peer, Flex Ltd (FLEX).

Starting with traditional valuation metrics, CLS appears overvalued compared to FLEX. Both PE, P/S, and EV/Sales ratios indicate that CLS’ stock is more expensive. The only metric that signals otherwise is the TTM PEG, which I favor because it accounts for the company’s growth potential. Both forward PEG ratios are very close.

In terms of growth, CLS anticipates significantly higher revenue growth than FLEX. The relatively low FWD PEG ratio for FLEX is mainly due to its low price rather than any significant EPS growth. Notably, FLEX anticipates EPS growth of 18.43%, compared to CLS at 51.06% — clearly highlighting the growth gap between the two companies in both revenue and EPS terms.

Finally, despite CLS’ low margins, its profitability remains stronger than FLEX, with higher gross, EBIT, and net profit margins. FLEX’s FCF margin is higher than CLS, which enhances my risk concerns regarding CLS’ low FCF and its ability to cost-effectively support expansion. However, CLS’ ROIC of 21.30% is far higher than FLEX’s 9.20%. I emphasize ROIC because, for growth-stage companies, how effectively investments are deployed is crucial for performance and financial health.

Overall, I believe that CLS has more promising growth potential compared to its main competitor. While traditional valuation metrics indicate an expensive stock, the FWD PEG and EPS growth reflect the true value that CLS holds. Given its superior profitability and ROIC, I strongly believe that CLS is undervalued.

Risks Analysis

In order to have a complete view of the company’s valuation, it is necessary to assess its risk factors.

As previously mentioned, my key concern is the company’s low margins. Although there has been margin expansion over the last few years, margins still remain at relatively low levels.

Another significant risk factor is the company’s high revenue concentration among a few clients. For FY24, two customers represented more than 10% of revenue, and the top 10 clients accounted for 73%. This concentration makes CLS sensitive to cancellations or delays from these key customers, which could materially affect revenue trajectory and overall performance.

While I anticipate that the company’s growing portfolio of customized solutions will attract more clients and increase customer stickiness, this concentration remains a meaningful risk.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Celestica’s stock appears undervalued given its growth catalysts and in comparison to its main competitor. Naturally, it's a high risk-reward stock—particularly in the short term—due to the risks of high revenue concentration, low margins, and even lower FCF margin.

Nevertheless, the company’s strong guidance suggests that, while these risks remain, CLS is managing them effectively. Additionally, its revenue guidance reflects strong momentum driven by customized products and solutions.

Overall, I rate Celestica’s stock a buy.

data.title: This Is Why December Could Flip My Rating From Sell To Buy
data.description:
November is about to end largely unchanged from the previous month for the S&P 500. Yet, despite the appearance of a "resilient bull market," it was actually a volatile month, with mega-cap tech stocks under pressure, then recovering, and liquidity starting to run low in the system. In short, it was anything but a calm month.

In this analysis, we'll recap what happened during November and offer a view for December.

Growth slows, value picks up again

As I mentioned, from a distance, the S&P 500 (SP500) appears to have shown resilience. The Nasdaq is less able to hide its weakness, posting a loss of more than 2% in November, thus breaking a streak of consecutive positive months.

Growth vs. Value

The comparison between growth and value stocks is very clear and shows how growth stocks have significantly underperformed, primarily due to the technology sector. Value stocks, on the other hand, are regaining ground. This type of movement suggests that the index didn't move unilaterally/directionally, but rather that there was a genuine rotation during the month. Investors preferred to reduce risk and shift it to more defensive assets.

Personally, I don't believe the market has entered a new long-term paradigm, but the signal is there, it's clear, and it must be read and analyzed as such to better understand the underlying situation. In my opinion, it reflects an awareness I've been repeating for several weeks: if valuations are so high, a single low will be enough to bring everything down in one fell swoop. Typically, the onset of such rotations indicates that the market is beginning to doubt the validity of these valuations.

Sectors

In terms of sectors, the market's shift from "euphoria" to "caution" mode is even more evident, with sectors like healthcare, consumer staples, and materials performing better than sectors like information technology, consumer discretionary, and industrials, which performed poorly during the month.

Volatility

Crowding, especially in light of the mega-cap tech earnings and the Fed's comments, was evident when looking at the options market as a whole. Indeed, as you can see from the chart below, the put/call ratio first soared and then suddenly fell. Essentially, we went from a euphoric market, filled with call options being bought in droves, to a much more neutral position in just a few days. What I'd like to emphasize, however, is the speed of the movement. The​‍​‌‍​‍‌ options market was extremely out of balance before the mega-cap earnings and the Fed's comments; a matter of fact, the put-call skew had even reversed, which is an indication that investors were paying a higher premium for calls than for puts. To put it another way, basically all players were on the same side, believing that big tech companies would keep delivering good results. ​‍​‌‍​

Indeed, yes, earnings performed very well, with the exception of a few stocks that disappointed. Despite this, it took very little, combined with a less dovish Fed commentary than expected, and the excess deflated in two sessions.

From here, we can sense the markets' nervousness. We're now at more normal levels, but the market has provided us with a clear reading, and we must acknowledge it.

The problem isn't the Fed, but liquidity.

At first glance, it would seem that the Fed's comments have changed the market narrative. Or Nvidia (NVDA), with its doubts about its balance sheet (inventories and loans). Or the news of Google's Tensor Processing Units, or TPUs (GOOGL), which caused some AI stocks to collapse and supported others. There were no significant macro data (due to the shutdown), so I can confirm that the real move, the one that split the month in two and created a rotation of sectors and investment styles, was liquidity. It's always happened: when liquidity dwindles, problems begin to emerge.

As I've stated in some of my recent analyses, in recent weeks/months the TGA has reached around €1 trillion. This was caused by the shutdown, which allowed the government to accumulate liquidity in the Treasury account and avoid spending it on the real economy (wages, agricultural subsidies, etc.).

Added to this is the issue of the "Standing Repo Facility." Banks aren't using the repo facility. Which is actually abnormal. It was specifically created by the Fed with the sole purpose of providing liquidity to the system at negligible cost when needed; yet, banks prefer to turn to the money market, which tends to be more expensive. The question is: why? The answer is: they don't want to appear weak in the eyes of the Fed.

Add to this the fact that bank reserves (another figure no one looks at until it's too late) have fallen to levels that historically coincide with fragile market phases.

All of this data combined increases market stress and its sensitivity to any setback. So forget about the Fed, quarterly reports, and AI. These aren't the causes of this month's stress. They're the excuses the market has found to correct itself. Let's first look at how dollars move within the system, and then talk about valuations, the Nasdaq, AI, and whatnot. But as long as gas is scarce, we won't get very far.

A more mature AI?

Let's forget about the AI ​​we've been talking about for the last twelve months. The one focused on GPUs and the "without Nvidia, you can't do AI." That world is changing. When an industry stops being monocentric and expands. What we saw in November are the first real indication that AI is moving to phase two. There are new players, new metrics, a new AI economy. (I made a article about Alphabet; if you want to know, it is a good piece to be read). This deepening of competitiveness is very important not only for Alphabet but also for the whole AI ecosystem, and in particular for Nvidia.

Why do I say this? Because the AI ​​industry today faces a problem that is often ignored: capex is growing at an almost unsustainable rate, far faster than companies' ability to generate returns.

The following chart (capex vs. cash flow for hyperscalers) illustrates what I'm talking about. In ten years, we've gone from a "normal" ratio to a point where, by 2026, capital expenditures could absorb nearly 80% of the aggregate cash flow of Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Oracle. This isn't a situation that can last forever. It's financial stress. Of course, I'm also convinced that spending will somehow normalize sooner or later. But how? Maybe we've found the solution.

The real risk of AI isn't the bubble, but...

The risk isn't an "AI bubble." The risk is financial saturation. That's why spending needs to normalize and companies need to start cashing in. That's why Google, AWS, Microsoft, and Meta's entry with alternative chips to GPUs is crucial. Not because they'll "kill Nvidia" (spoiler: they won't), but because they lower system temperatures.

Let me be clear: I'm not saying TPUs are a valid alternative to GPUs. Absolutely not. Nvidia's chips are the most general-purpose, much more versatile, but also more expensive and, in some ways, more energy-hungry. Other manufacturers, however, focus on single-task chips, which are therefore less versatile but also less expensive. In recent versions, they have also managed to be much more energy efficient.

Google has been producing these chips for 10 years, but only recently have they come back into the spotlight, as many (including META) are considering using them instead of Nvidia's (or even diversifying) to use them in their own data centers.

"The American DeepSeek"

We can see Alphabet's TPUs as the American DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK). The first real system shock. They highlighted the fact that it is possible to create cutting-edge AI without buying only Nvidia GPUs. The most absurd fact (I call it the "AI paradox") is that this competition is primarily beneficial to Nvidia. That​‍​‌‍​‍‌ might sound quite ridiculous, but it is actually the fact. The industry dominated by Nvidia, on which everyone depends in one way or another, is way less stable than the one where Nvidia is the top player, however, there are other companies providing the same ​‍​‌‍​‍‌product.

  • If​‍​‌‍​‍‌ hyperscalers providers faces a decline in profits, which company will reduce its orders first? Nvidia.
  • If cash flow is not enough to meet expenses, which company is at the risk of not being paid on time? Nvidia.
  • If the cost of the industry increases significantly, which company is blamed for "stifling the AI ​​cycle"? ​‍​‌‍​‍‌Nvidia.

So, believe me: the​‍​‌‍​‍‌ risk is not competition. The risk is un-sustainability. A more competitive but stable market is preferable to a unmanageable monopoly.

Conclusion

As of this writing, the market appears to be positioning itself for a 25 basis point rate cut in December (80% probability). Unfortunately, the BLS will not release macroeconomic data before the FOMC meeting; these data will arrive later due to the shutdown. Also keep an eye on Oracle's quarterly results: they could provide more insight into the company's health and the health of the AI ​​sector overall.

Returning to the Fed, the surprise could be the return of QE. As I've said in the past, the Fed typically moves in three phases:

  1. Rate cuts
  2. End of QT
  3. Start of QE.

This is the cycle. We're in the second phase; only the third is missing to complete the picture. If it doesn't happen in December, it will happen in January, but it's something that will inevitably happen because it has become necessary. When the liquidity system tightens like this, the Fed has few options. It must intervene. And the only quick way is to buy Treasuries. Call it QE, call it what you will, but the essence is the same.

I​‍​‌‍​‍‌ will simply change my mind if the Federal Reserve restarts purchases even if it is just for a small amount. Without a second thought, I will move my rating from "Sell" to "Buy." It's a matter of oxygen. The market will be different if cash inflow starts ​‍​‌‍​‍‌again.

That's why I say December won't be a game of "what will Powell say on the microphone?" The real game will be what happens behind the scenes. If even a minimal easing occurs:

  • financing calms down,
  • reserves increase,
  • pressure on indices eases,
  • and the AI ​​theme can continue to grow without slowing down (or at least without a crash).

December could be the watershed moment we've all been waiting for. From a struggling market to a market that's finally starting to breathe again. If the Fed really does what I think, Santa's present will be under the tree.

Thanks for reading. If you like, feel free to leave a comment with your opinions on this market.

data.title: Siemens Energy: At An All-Time-High, My Rating Remains Hold
data.description:
If there's one flaw I'd ascribe to myself when it comes to investing, it's underestimating the potential for market irrationality, or how "good growth can get" in a company, even if the underlying earnings do not support it. I'm too much of a hardcore valuation investor to ignore facts and earnings. Sometimes this leads to exiting investments too early (relative to what they achieve), or underestimating reversal or growth potential in a company.

Siemens Energy AG (OTCPK:SMEGF, FWB: ENR) is a good example of this, especially what has happened to the company as of late. Since my last article, Siemens Energy is up almost 80%, against a market that's up around 30%. It's a clear outperformance. Part of it is certainly because the company has turned around things better and faster than expected, but I will continue to argue that a big part of it is continued exuberance.

In this article, I will attempt a balanced view and update for this company, to see what it could offer, and where it could go from here. But one thing's clear - if you are looking for someone to tag along to the next "growth story," even if that growth is exuberant, I'm not your guy. I'm more the investor who invests in things when they are dirt cheap, willing to hold for years until a turnaround.

When it comes to Siemens Energy, I exited early because at the time the stock was divested, it wasn't a good or appealing company.

That's something that has changed a bit.

The latest results were reported about 2 weeks ago, finalizing the 2025A fiscal. Because of excellent performance and fulfilling all goals, it's not surprising that we've seen impressive growth in the stock and, in a big way, not something that I had expected of the company, to be sure.

Most recommendations for the company here are positive. Let me show you why I'm raising my target and my expectations, but why I remain at least somewhat skeptical about the broadly-painted positive thesis we have here.

Siemens Energy Q4 '25 - Good results, good quarter, good forecasts - but at what price?

I could show you my problems with Siemens Energy in one slide or picture, but this would perhaps be too simplistic given the returns we're seeing. So I feel that I have to explain it a bit more.

Let's start with Q4 '25.

The company's full-year results fully justify an increased confidence, or upside, if you will. With solid order growth, record backlogs, margin growth of over 500 bps, and favorable pricing trends across the company, I can't find anything to criticize about this quarter - or indeed about the full year by the company. For the next year, the company is expecting yet another double-digit rate of growth. But what is most interesting is what the company expects in terms of margins. 6% in this year (4.5% in Q4) is expected to rise to, or above 10% in 2026, and to 15-16% in 2028E.

The company also finally introduced its dividend again. This was only a matter of time, if the company turned around, but given that it was 63% more than I expected, the company does get "points" for this. Still, the company's argument in Q4 '25 is that the share price rose over 200% in a year - and this is true.

But it's also 200% from a level of essentially very low, and the 500 bps margin increase - that was from a negative profit margin. So while I like what I see, I'm not as quick to laud the company for everything of this, because I recall selling before things turned down horribly in Siemens Energy some years ago. The stock was a loser for well over 3 years, and I don't regret selling that (even if this is the result), because during that time I've made good returns.

But I did miss out on "entering back in" - and that needs to be made clear.

In a sense, I am trying to be objective here. I'm not trying to take away things from the company, but I'm also not letting readers, or the company, forget the journey that brought the business here. Siemens Energy was not an impressive name for a long time.

Summarizing, the company saw growth in virtually all segments and regions, and there is little in the company's current KPI's that gives indications of trouble.

So why am I not raising the company to what some analysts would consider a well-deserved "Buy"? What about the guidance raise, and what about the consensus until then?

I argue that part of the reason for outperformance is not good 2025A results, but in fact lofty 2028E estimates - and when it comes to those, I am doubtful. These increases are, like much today, based on AI/tech demand, specifically increased demand for gas for things like data centers and the ongoing coal-to-gas transition, which is partially driven by these trends. There is no doubt in my mind that the company now deserves a far higher fair value than it currently has, based on my last article, in addition to the lifting of the dividend restriction by the German government in 2024.

Maybe it's also because I'm still a bit burned by the 2020-2023 period (though I sold at a profit early), with issues that were at the center of the company's valuation and issues for a long time. Bulls would now point to things like the modernization of the grid and the technologies associated with this (like coal-to-gas, as mentioned), which creates demand for Siemens Energy products. This is one of the reasons I'm raising the target.

We could also argue for data center upside - that's another reason I'm increasing my upside - at least somewhat. Management doesn't get points for turnaround from me - that was, in my view, something that should have been done far earlier. What this means is "normalization" for me, not premiumization. We're talking about a Siemens business. To me, that means that quality is a presumption, not an alternative for management.

So what problems do I see that cause me to stay conservative here to the degree that I am?

Just because the company has turned things around does not mean that risks are gone. And just because there's currently a high demand for power-intensive data centers based on AI needs does not mean that this increase is going to see linear or proportional growth. The efficiency of alternative models such as DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) could significantly reduce the demand for Siemens Energy products. Also, if anyone thinks Wind power issues are over, I would strongly disagree with that. Unforeseen repair costs for turbines are still here, and could increase due to faster product cycles - as I've pointed out before. Innovation in the segment is high (in terms of demand), because competition is high. And if the company turns back to losses or negative, further cash injections and cap raises could become necessary (though I admit that the last one is a remote risk).

So while I acknowledge the positives here, I'm not the type of analyst to shift everything about a company because of a few positives.

Let's see what valuation tells us here.

Valuation for Siemens Energy

Do you think that an energy company should trade at close to 60x P/E?

Because if you don't, then Siemens Energy might not be for you.

I say this because Siemens Energy's current average weighted valuation is over 58x P/E normalized.

But, you might say, both the company and analysts are expecting significant growth going forward. Just look at that - by 2028, earnings will be 4-5x the current AEPS level, and then the valuation won't even be 30 or 25x, if considered like that.

Sure - but can we really consider this as gospel or likely? The company expects it, for sure. Even analysts seem to in part be on board with this. But let's remember that historically, forecasting this company has been impossible. With a 10% margin of error, forecasting the company has been missed both negatively and positively 100% of the time over the last 5 years. So declaring that we "Know" what's going to happen in the next 3 with this sort of conviction is quite ambitious - and I wouldn't want to do so.

Sure, using a 42x P/E average for the 5-year and using current growth estimates implies a 100%+ RoR even from this level.

This is a BBB-rated company. I no longer believe the company to be "very risky," as before. This is because Gamesa is now, at least for the time being, solved. Profitability in 2024, better in 2025. Breakeven is possible for 2026E, or even higher. But the current valuation targets are entirely, in my view, disregarding the cyclical nature of the company.

Unlike other analysts, I do penalize management a bit for the issues and for how long they persisted in Gamesa, while at the same time acknowledging it was a tricky legacy to inherit.

My previous fair value of around €45/share with a price target, or PT, of €30 or below to make up for the risk I saw is here raised to a fair value of €60/share, with a PT of €45/share. The fair value target represents a 25-30x P/E average for the next few years, which, in my view, is the highest possible target I'd want to give the company.

Analyst averages for Siemens Energy here are telling of how the volatility is in the company. The low-end target from 23 analysts is €37/share, lower even than my previous fair value target. The high-end target is €170/share. I have not previously observed such a significant variation between high-end and low-end targets, and I think this expresses a significant uncertainty and volatility for the company's future.

Based on this, I don't consider it too conservative to only give a PT of €45 and a fair value of €60/share. I am not changing my rating from Hold.

Risks for Siemens Energy here are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

This one is a bit of a learning opportunity. I underestimated the earnings growth potential and recovery of Siemens Energy - but I also believe the market is significantly overestimating the impact of EPS growth for the next few years. I believe the next few years of EPS growth will be a lot less than is currently forecasted, owing to demand risk and volatility, as well as legacy wind power repair costs.

If the company were to actually manage this upside, then the resulting EPS growth and potential could imply more than 100% TSR potential - but I would, once again, view that with a bit of uncertainty.

I have my new share price target of €45/share for the company, translating to ADR pricing of $52/share. Keep in mind that the current state of FX means the company is very differently priced in USD than in EUR.

Thesis

  • The company was one of the more interesting plays in all of Europe, on a mix of legacy gas/power as well as a massive renewable operation, it seeks to pull from the public markets. After excessively positive reactions or overreactions, as I consider them, the market is not showing me an attractively priced Siemens Energy. This means I'm negative on the business prospects for this company, and would be more careful than before. I am upgrading my targets, but sticking to my previous target of Hold.
  • I'm also sticking to my Hold rating, and I'm not adding shares of Siemens Energy back to my portfolio. My price point goes up to €45/share, with a fair value target of €60, but I want to see what the company does in terms of recovery and other things going forward before I initiate a new position in the stock. At this point, I would also want a substantially lower price.
  • For those reasons, I'm going to stick to my conservative here, and I believe investors should consider doing the same - and this is my stance for 2026E.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them:

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 2/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. I give the company a Hold rating as of November 2025 with a PT of €45/share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kinross Gold: Don't Chase The Stock Here
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. MTPA = million tonnes per annum. FS/DFS = Definitive Feasibility Study. Operating costs refer to production cost of sales, not all-in sustaining costs.

Introduction

Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC) is a multi-asset gold producer with three operations in the United States (Alaska, Nevada), and its other three operations in Mauritania, Chile, and Brazil. The company's largest two assets which contribute just shy of 60% of annual production are in Brazil and West Africa, with these being Paracatu and Tasiast. As for reserves, the company has ~21.9 million ounces in reserves, albeit at grades well below the 500,000+ ounce producer peer average (0.77 G/T of gold vs. ~1.90 G/T of gold).

This is primarily related to the Paracatu, that's been operating commercially since 1987 (but artisanal mining dates back over 300 years with placer gold found in the Paracatu region); is one of the largest assets globally by throughput at ~60 MTPA, but which is a large-scale lower-grade deposit. This significant lower-grade reserve base (~4.89 million ounces at 0.40 G/T of gold) drags down Kinross' average reserve grade, as does its lower-grade Fort Knox Mine in Alaska.

Following what has been a strong delivery year-to-date vs. guidance (~1.53 million ounces of gold produced at $1,490/oz AISC), Kinross has enjoyed a solid year-to-date performance, gaining over 190% year-to-date which complements an already impressive 50% return last year. That said, the stock has found itself running straight into a 30-year resistance level coupled with a short-term technical sell signal that severely increases the probability of rallies being sold into.

(*) Full-year 2025 guidance is ~2.0 million ounces at ~$1,500/oz AISC (*).

If we combine this with the fact that Kinross offers no real margin of safety at current levels, I see this as a dangerous point to be chasing the stock here above US$27.50. This is because Kinross is now trading at just shy of 14x FY2026 EV/FCF estimates ($3,700/oz gold price assumption in 2026) and at a premium to most of its major producer peers despite a lower growth profile (2025 to 2029), I see this as the least attractive reward/risk setup for Kinross since August 2020, its prior peak ahead of a multi-quarter correction.

Often, when the time comes to trim positions and re-balance, few want to, and while KGC could certainly go higher, I see this as an opportune time to be booking some additional profits after what's been a near parabolic ~2000% rally in the stock (2016 low to 2025 highs), now roughly matching its run from its 2000 bear market lows to 2008 highs.

Q3 Production & Sales

Kinross released its Q3 '25 results earlier this month, reporting quarterly production of ~503,900 gold-equivalent ounces, a 10% decline from the year-ago period. Lower production was related to lower grades at Tasiast, lower grades at Fort Knox (reduced contribution from Manh Choh satellite), and lower production from its Nevada mines, partially offset by higher grades at Paracatu and La Coipa.

Fortunately, Kinross remains well on track to meet guidance despite the softer year-over-year, and this would mark its third consecutive year of beating its guidance midpoint, if achieved, a material improvement from three consecutive misses in the 2020 to 2022 period.

As for its proportion of production from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions, this figure slid to just below 35% vs. 36% in the year-ago period, leaving Kinross as one of the producers with the lowest proportion of production coming from safer and highly regarded Tier-1 jurisdictions. Let's take a look at production by mine:

Quarterly Production by Mine

Fort Knox

Starting with Fort Knox, production came in at ~95,700 ounces at operating costs of $1,359/oz, up from $958/oz in the year-ago period. Lower production year-over-year was attributed to a decline in mill grades (1.86 G/T of gold vs. 4.03 G/T of gold) and lower recoveries, impacted by the ramp-up of Phase 10 ore and lower contribution from the high-grade Manh Choh satellite deposit (only ~286,000 tonnes from Manh Choh in Q3'25). As for cost performance, higher costs were related to fewer ounces sold and higher labor and maintenance supply costs.

On a positive note, Fort Knox is having a solid year despite the planned lower production year-over-year in Q3'25, with ~339,000 ounces produced year-to-date and operating earnings more than doubling year-over-year to ~$475 million in the first nine months of the year (Q1-Q3'24: ~$222 million).

La Coipa

As for its La Coipa Mine, production improved to ~58,000 ounces at costs of $1,199/oz, up from ~50,500 gold-equivalent ounces at $1,074/oz in Q3'24. Higher production was related to higher throughput (~932,000 tonnes) and gold grades (2.36 G/T of gold vs. 2.17 G/T of gold), with operating earnings surging to ~$93 million in Q3'25 vs. ~$34 million in the year-ago period. As for performance vs. guidance, La Coipa will need a strong Q4 to meet its guidance of 230,000 ounces with it sitting at just ~164,500 ounces year-to-date.

As for cost performance, Kinross called out higher royalties, labor and maintenance supply costs, with year-to-date costs sitting at $1,242/oz, nearly $200/oz above its 2025 guidance of $1,060/oz. That said, Kinross deserves a pass here given that cost guidance this year was provided at much lower gold prices with the company budgeting for costs based on a $2,500/oz gold price vs. a gold price that has averaged closer to $3,450/oz year-to-date. Hence, while many producers have missed their cost guidance in 2025, it has largely been because of factors outside their control (primarily increased costs due to higher royalties).

Paracatu

Moving to Kinross' largest mine, Paracatu, production came in at ~150,400 ounces at $933/oz, up from ~146,200 ounces at $1,006/oz in the year-ago period. Higher output was related to increased grades as mining moved into higher-grade ore, partially offset by lower throughput (~13.2 million tonnes vs. ~14.6 million tonnes) due to increased ore hardness that affected mill throughput. Notably, costs were lower year-over-year despite higher royalty costs and contractor costs, and Paracatu is tracking well against guidance of 585,000 ounces with ~80% of planned output delivered year-to-date (~466,300 ounces vs. 585,000 ounces guided).

As for profitability, Paracatu had a phenomenal quarter, generating ~$323 million of operating earnings, with earnings from this asset doubling year-over-year (Q3 '24: ~$159 million). Looking ahead to Q4 '25, we should see another 130,000+ ounce quarter with the benefit of record gold prices, pushing annual operating earnings from this mine upwards of $1.0 billion in 2025.

Tasiast

Finally, Kinross' Tasiast Mine produced ~120,900 ounces at $889/oz costs, down from ~158,500 ounces at $688/oz in the year-ago period. Lower production from this massive African asset (~9MTPA throughput rate) was related to lower throughput and grades (1.78 G/T of gold vs. 2.46 G/T of gold), partially offset by higher recoveries because of optimization work. Lower grades were as expected but significantly affected production and costs, with Tasiast's high-grade West Branch 4 ore blended with low-grade stockpiles.

As it stands, Kinross is heading out of West Branch 4 ore with a focus on development of West Branch 5 to bring high-grade back ore back into the mine plan in the 2027 to 2029 period. Unfortunately, this will weigh on production and costs over the next several quarters when combined with higher royalties, with a 6.5% royalty payable to the Government of Mauritania and a 2.0% royalty payable to Franco-Nevada (FNV).

Given what's likely to be lower production at Tasiast in 2026, Kinross should see much higher costs year-over-year when combined with the headwind of higher royalties. This is because Tasiast is the company's lowest cost operation, with lower contribution from this asset being a minor drag on costs, as well as higher royalties. The good news is that Tasiast has a very bright future ahead, and it should be easier to justify a potential UG component to this mine to fill in the 2030 production profile given what will be a rapid payback if a $3,000/oz+ gold price environment is here to stay.

The latter is similar to what B2Gold has pursued at Fekola, with it able to pick up some of the slack from a grade standpoint with the best years of OP grades behind it, benefiting from ~4.5 G/T Fekola UG ore (70,000+ ounces per year assuming a 1,500 TPD contribution rate).

Financial Results

As for Kinross' financial results, revenue came in at ~$1,802 million (+26% year-over-year), cash flow surged to ~$1,024 million (+40% year-over-year) and attributable free cash flow increased to ~$687 million, translating to ~38% free cash flow margins, a 900+ basis point improvement year-over-year. Given the significant free cash flow generation combined with the sale of its Asante stake (inherited from the Chirano Mine sale), Kinross exited Q3 '25 with ~$1.72 billion in cash and cash equivalents and nearly $500 million in net cash despite ~$165 million spent on share repurchases in Q3 '25 alone.

Looking ahead to Q4 '25, Kinross should enjoy an average realized gold price of $4,100/oz or better, setting up another massive quarter for free cash flow generation with it likely to exit the year with ~$1.0 billion in net cash despite what's expected to be aggressive share repurchases and its recently increased quarterly dividend ($0.035 vs. $0.03), with increased cash flow partially offset by higher capex spend in Q4 '25.

This net cash position will benefit further from the sale of additional Asante shares, with Kinross noting that it exercised its conversion on its 2032 convertible debt into Asante shares and subsequently sold these ~62 million shares for net proceeds of ~$96 million.

As for the bigger picture standpoint and even assuming more conservative gold price assumptions of $3,700/oz next year, Kinross should generate ~$2.2 billion in free cash flow next year, setting the company to exit 2026 with close to $3.0 billion in net cash, assuming only modest share repurchases. This is a great position to be in ahead of a major build later this decade, Great Bear, which could cost closer to $1.6 billion in upfront capex (starting in 2027 and subject to permit timing) adjusted for inflation.

Costs & Margins

Looking at costs and margins, Kinross' all-in sustaining costs came in at $1,622/oz, up 20% year-over-year. Higher AISC was related to increased sustaining capital spend (~$179 million) and the impact of higher royalties and maintenance supply costs at several assets. Fortunately, this was more than offset by the higher gold price with AISC margins climbing to $1,836/oz (~58.1%), up from $1,129/oz (~45.5%) in the year-ago period.

As for Kinross' delivery vs. guidance, FY2025 AISC are sitting at $1,490/oz YTD vs. guidance of $1,500/oz and I wouldn't be surprised to see AISC land above $1,500/oz this year and slightly miss its midpoint. That said, and as noted earlier, this is largely related to higher royalties with solid cost control given that the gold price (XAUUSD:CUR) outperformed its budget by ~$900/oz for estimating costs this year.

As for how margins stacked up with peers, Kinross' AISC margins lagged that of Newmont (NEM), but Kinross had a higher capex quarter from a sustaining capital standpoint vs. Newmont severely underspent on sustaining capex vs. initial plans. Hence, when adjusting for these differences, Kinross' AISC margins were actually among the highest vs. its large-scale producer peers.

Recent Developments

Curlew

As for its Curlew Project in Washington, Kinross noted that initial development of the Roadrunner exploration decline is complete, as well as North Stealth development, allowing for better drill access of the high-grade Roadrunner target and potential extensions to mineralization at North Stealth. This asset could see a production decision approved next year, with Curlew potentially supporting a ~100,000 ounce production profile and helping to offset what's likely to be a lower production profile post-2029 at Tasiast.

Great Bear

Moving to its cornerstone development project, Great Bear, this asset in Ontario, Canada will be a game-changer for the company with the ability to bring online ~500,000 high-margin gold ounces per annum at industry-leading AISC below $950/oz (*) in its first eight years of higher production (~518,000 ounces). Kinross spent ~$28 million at Great Bear in Q3 '25 and has spent over ~$76 million year-to-date, noting that its Main Project remains on schedule with detailed engineering for the mill, TMF and other site infrastructure underway.

(*) Kinross' LOM AISC estimate of $812/oz was based on a PEA, which generally understates operating costs, and a gold price of $1,900/oz, with a $40/oz impact from royalties alone at $3,900/oz gold. (*)

Unfortunately, while the natural gas pipeline has been commissioned and its camp is operational as part of its AEX (Advanced Exploration) program, the company has only received three of five permits (two water permits remain outstanding), with it previously noting that it hoped to receive this before year-end. This has resulted in a minor slip in the schedule for the AEX program, with the excavation of the exploration decline not expected to start until summer 2026.

Although this is a minor disappointment, the goal of the AEX program is to extract a bulk sample and complete additional definition/infill drilling of UG material in the coming years. Importantly, this minor slip in schedule because of delayed permits does not impede the start of the main project. This is because the bulk of material fed to the future ~3.7 MTPA mill is open-pit material in the first few years, including a portion of 2029 feed from stockpiles/open-pit material.

Share Repurchases

As for deploying cash from its record free cash flow generation, Kinross has shared that besides redeeming its $500 million in 2027 Senior Notes in December, it had repurchased ~$405 million in shares up to early November vs. no repurchases completed in 2023 and 2024. This included ~8.8 million shares repurchased in Q3 '25 alone, and the company has upped its buyback program to $600 million minimum for 2025, suggesting another ~$195 million in share repurchases in the last seven weeks of the year.

Although this is certainly one way to return capital to shareholders vs. increasing the base dividend further or paying a special dividend, I'm a little surprised at the level of buybacks which seem to be more predicated on its financial position than the valuation of the stock. Personally, I prefer those companies that repurchase shares opportunistically to those that are buying back shares with less attention paid to valuations and are not price sensitive, but obviously the market sees things differently than me given how well KGC has performed.

To be clear, KGC is not expensive at over 14x FY2026 free cash flow estimates ($3,700/oz gold), but it's not cheap either and I would have much preferred to this level of aggressive buybacks below US$8.00/share vs. waiting for a 600% rally in the stock (US$3.00 ----> US$21.00+) to deploy record sums of capital into these share buybacks.

Valuation

Based on ~1.21 billion shares and a share price of US$28.20, Kinross trades at a market cap of ~$34.1 billion and an enterprise value of ~$33.6 billion. This makes Kinross one of the most expensive producers of its scale in the market today on a valuation per ounce produced basis compared to other peers with less than 50% of production coming from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions.

In fact, Kinross now trades at over $17,000 per ounce of 2026 gold production, significantly above one of its closest peers by scale, Northern Star (OTCPK:NESRF), at ~$14,000/oz. This is despite Northern Star being as Tier-1 only jurisdiction producer with far stronger growth rates, which should leave it trading at a premium to Kinross. Meanwhile, Kinross trades at more than double the 2026 free cash flow of B2Gold (BTG) and nearly three times its value on a market cap per ounce produced basis, with B2Gold trading at barely ~$6,000 per ounce of 2026 gold production.

So, from purely a relative value standpoint, Kinross has rarely ever been less attractive at current levels. Further, if we look at where the stock has traded historically, Kinross has typically peaked near cash flow over the past decade. Today, Kinross now finds itself trading back at over ~9x cash flow using a base case assumption of $3,700/oz gold price assumption for 2026, meaning it is far more gold price reliant than peers given it was already seen considerable multiple expansion from when it was buried below 3.5x cash flow at dirt-cheap levels in 2022.
